CURRENT AFFAIRS,

»

The ““ Freeman,”” with a recklessness that is remark-
able, called its leading article recently ‘“ The Devolu-
tionists: Again.”” We thought that the Convention had
ignominiously ‘kicked out the attenuated * Devolution’
Bill brought in by Mr. Birrell on behalf of the
““Liberal ” Government which the Irish vote in Eng-
land helped to place in its present position. Birrell’s
““ Devolution ’’ Bill proposed no powers, restricted or
unrestricted, in the way of legislation.  One would
have thought that the ‘Freeman’’ would, with a
cautious sensitiveness to damaged cases, have refrained
from stimulating thought on the Birrell bungle. But
““The Devolutionists Again’’ article refers not to Bir-
rell’s bungle, but to the meeting of Lord Dunraven and
the Reform Association.

Whatever we may think of the Reform Association—
and it is rather milk and water—it, at least, stands
higher on the political stock exchange than the Irish
Parliamentary Party, who were practically kicked and
humiliated by the Convention amid prolonged cheers.
The ‘‘Freeman’’ says:—‘ The supplied report (the
report of the proceedings of Lord Dunraven’s Associa-
tion) shows that the meeting on Tuesday was a sort of
wake over the Irish Council Bill.”” Indeed! But the

“tences of the ‘‘ I'reeman’’ article. .

Trish Council Bill was the ‘“ Liberal ”’ Bill brought in
by the party so largely supported by the Irish vote in
Tingland. Where is the sense of trying to make a sad
joke about Dunraven ‘‘waking’’ a ““Liberal”’ Bill
that the Convention kicked out amid cheers! The
““ T'reeman ”’ says:—‘‘ On the one hand the Association
wished to repudiate paternity for the rejected Bill, and
on the other to convince the public that the country
has made a woful mistake in rejecting the imperfect
and unworkable scheme.” We certainly have no brief
for the Reform Association, but neither are we partisans
of the ‘‘Freeman,”’ and the ‘‘Freeman,”’ even if it
and the Irish Parliamentary Party are in a corner,
might be more careful in its writing. Surely the Irish
Party had something to do with Birrell’s bungle, and
if they hadn’t, it appears to us that they neglected the
chief business for which they ostensibly were in Par-
liament. We read the report of the meeting
of the Reform Association, and in justice to that
Association we must say that the “ Freeman *’ is utterly
misleading in the latter part of the sentence that we
have quoted. There was nothing in the report advocat-
ing the adoption of the ‘imperfect and unworkable
scheme ’’ which the ‘‘ Freeman’’ and the Irish Party
were offered by their friends—the ‘‘ Liberal ’’ Govern-
ment. There was talk of its imperfections, and talk
of the advisability of attempting to amend it.

The ‘‘IFreeman’ finds it necessary to go into a
wrangle as to the respective merits of the Dunraven
proposals and the Birrell offer; that fact in itself is 2
rather severe criticism of the forces represented by the
Irish Party on the floor of the House, for Birrell’s oifer
was, in a way, the result of the Irish Party’s pressure.
The Convention, amid loud cheers, spurned Birrell’s
offer, and Mr. Redmond, having scouted the idea of the
party being able to procure satisfactory amendments to
Birrell’s Billeen, asked the Convention—and the Con-
vention acceded to his request—to send him and his
colleagues back to the floor to get a full Parliament!
In the circumstances the ‘‘ Freeman’’ makes points
against the Reform Association as the Scotchman is
supposed to make jokes with difficulties.

The “ Freeman ’’ says:—‘‘ Manifestly the production
and rejection of a scheme of administrative devolution
is felt to be awkward for the apostles of the principle.
The country has now got the complete measure of the
policy, and the promoters feel that their position is
rudely shaken.”” This is rather mixed. Who are the
““ promoters?’’  Are they not the Irish Parliamentary
Party and the  Freeman?' Certainly -as a result of
Birrell’s bungle the Irish Party are ‘‘ rudely shaken.”

We are in thorough agreement with the last two sen-
They are as fol-
lows:—“ If the Reform Association wishes to promote
a union of classes in Ireland let it begin by inducing the
class that it represents to recognise that the country
of their birth is a nation with the rights of a nation.
They will find no barrier between themselves and their
countrymen when they are ready with that profession of
faith.”” Quite so. But might we also add a hope that
the ‘ Freeman ’’ and many of its friends will set about
recognising ‘‘ that the country of their birth is.a
nation with the rights of a nation.”” i

Under' the heading ‘‘ Constructive Policy’’ a cor-
respondent sent us an article suggesting that the Irish
Parliamentary Party should introduce a Bill of their
own leading up to the full measure. The advice is, on
the face of it, belated. =~ Cheering several times, the
Convention kicked out Birrell’s Bill and sent back, at

Mr. Redmond’s own suggestion, the Party, to worry for
‘Flome Rule. The Irish Party has now no ‘‘ mandate’’
o take even 19s. 11d. in the £—it asked and roceived
a mandate to press for the full amount.  Excited
people are now simmering down, but it is too late. The
Party have got a ““ mandate’” from the Convention to
press for Fome Rule, and they could not accept less
without coming back for a fresh mandate.

Tt is too late now to write as our correspondent
writes. ““ But let the Party introduce a Bill embody-
ing the instalment which we expected the Government
to bring in, which we may hope John Redmond re-
commended, and which the Liberals can reasonably and
honourably be expected to pass.’” The time for doing
that, as we suggested, was at the Convontion. The Bill
should have been amended at the Convention, and need-
less to say you can ‘“amend '’ a Bill to such an oxtent
that its author would not recognise it. Instead of kick-
ing out the Bill, the audience standing to their feet and
cheering several times, the Convention should have given
the Party a mandate as to the kind of Bill they would
accept as an instalment. The country is now suffering
from the effects of its glorious ‘“spree’” of  truly
national ** champagne.  Cheering several times, the
Convention gave the Party, at Mr. Redmond’s own sug-
gestion, a mandate to press for a native Parliament
with a responsible Executive, so that it is useless for
peaple waking up to reason after their wild ““ burst ’’ of
“ flinging it back in their teeth ’’ to be suggesting that
the Party should now bring in a half or three-quarter
way Bill of their own. We are gloriously committed by
the Convention to a full Parliament or nothing; it
seems to us that in the Home Rule line ‘“ Nothing ”’ is
what the betting tipster of the self-styled ‘‘ Nationalist
organ,”’ the Independent, might call “a dead cert.”
for some time to come.

The chief event of the year, so far, from the Irish-
Ireland standpoint, was the remarkable Clongowes Feis,
which took place last Sunday. It was a great day for
TIrish-Treland and for nation-building. The band of
the Irish Volunteers discoursed sweet Irish music, and
there was a great scene of excitement, the entire
audience at the Feis rising to their feet and cheering
several times, when the band wound up with the Irish
National Anthem. The Lord Chief Baron addressed
the audience in excellent Irish, and Dr. Joseph
McGrath, the well-known and spirited patriot, one of
the secretaries of the Royal University, a man who has
earned immortality by his boldness and courage, and
who is mortally dreaded by Sir James Creed Meredith,
announced that a letter of apology was received from,
amongst others, Mr. John E. Redmond, M.P., ““the
Ieader of the Irish race here to-day.”” Dr. McGrath,
the present day ‘‘ Liberator,” was re-elected ITon. Sec.
of the Clongowes Feis. The Chief Baron said ‘‘ That it
was to Dr. McGrath more than to any other person in
existence that ——,”” and so on, whilst the present day
‘“ Liberator,”” the terror of every enemy of Catholic
interests, declared that the success of the Feis ‘‘ was
not due to him,”” and so on. The success of this Clon-
gowes Feis is more than compensation for the petty
disappointment, created as a result of Birrell’s Bill. TFor
15 is an undoubted fact that it is to the spirit generated
by this great organisation associated with the Clongowes
College that we are indebted for the oxistence of the
great Trish University that is now causing the country
to throb with intellectual life. =~ When time-servers,
trimmers, and tames were nibbling at and resoluting
about the great University grievance years ago, it was
the clarion note and hold and fearless action of the men
of the Clongowes Union, led on by the ‘ Liberator,”

THE LEADER. :
Dr. McGrath, that cowed ‘“Trinity”’ and brought the °

JuNE 8, 1907.

Lnglish Government of that day to its knees. Those

of us who enjoy, without hurt to our consciences, the
blessings of University facilities to-day should not forget .
that it was Clongowes Union that “ rose in dark and

ovil days’’ and drove not a mere wedge but a hatchet
through the “ Trinity * monopoly ; we should remember
that if Tony Traill trembles and turns blue at the very
mention of the words ‘‘ Clongowes Union,”” that the
gallant men led on by our immortal ““Liberator,” Dr.
McGrath, did not inspire that salutary fear in the
hearts of our enemies without an expenditure of nerve
and intellect, and, above all, tactics Lhat were well-
nigh superhuman.

The Clongowes Feis was from every point of view the
great Trish-Ireland and nation-making event of 1907.
The well-known Irish-Ireland entertainers, the Cigzans,

went down specially from Dublin, and profoundly -

astonished the youngsters of Clongowes by their feats
in the ancient games and tricks of Ireland. Tt is a trite
saying now that the battles of Irish frecdom are fought
on the playgrounds of Clongowes College.

Tor the benefit of new readers who may see the
Leaper for the first time this week perhaps we had
better state that the above notes on Clongowes Union
are ironic.

The Sunday opening of Humphrey’s Dump is pulling
that unhappy enterprise into fresh kettles of boiling
water. The Protestant Rector of Donnybrook says that-
‘It will, moreover, add a new difficulty in the way of
Irishmen uniting in a common work, which, they are
agreed, is for the good of the country, if a majority sets
at nought the religious convictions of a minority.” If
we happened to be favourable to Humphrey’s Dump, we
would have some incisive things to say on that pro-
nouncement from the Protestant Rector of Donnybrook,
but we will let it pass, as we are of opinion that the
Dump should be closed every day as well as Sunday.
The Protestant Rector of Donnybrook, in a further
letter, says—‘‘ We believe that in no part of the United
Kingdom outside the Southern half of Ireland would
Sunday opening be permitted.”’  Pity the poor
““ Southern half!”” But any reply to this sort of thing
would be regarded as ‘‘ sectarian!”’ And the eloquent
Mr. Devlin might wither us with a ‘‘ standing here
to-day ’’ speech if we dared to say anything more! By
the way, we have not yet visited Humphrey’s Dump.
We intend going there this week on a visit of inspection.

The very important question of Irish spelling reform,
which has been engaging the attention of several of our
contributors, is taken up this week by Rev. Dr. O’Daly,
an acknowledged authority on phonetics, and a very dis-
tinguished linguist. Those of our readers who are in
touch with Irish language matters need no introduction
to Dr. O’Daly.

An influential correspondent, in the course of a pex-
sonal letter to us, writes:—‘ Advise T. W. Russell, in-
stead of running counter to Irish prejudices like Sir
1. Plunkett, to make concessions to them; as St. Patrick
himself deemed it expedient to do, otherwise he is sure
to fail.  We will not on any account develop on English
lines—socially, religiously, morally, intellectually, econo-
mically, educationally or politically; and until the
powers that be put this in their pipe and smoke it,
they’ll be only wasting their time trying their panaceas
on us, and are bound to be as big failures as the Scotch
farmers who came to Munster 40 years ago were, and
against whose very methods the land itself actually
rebelled.’”’

JUNE 8, 1907.

~ Qur old friend, Viggo Schwartz, is now manager of
“The Danish Produce Export Company, Lbd.g,” of
: Copenhagen. . We have a circular letter from this firm
before us. Viggo is pushing the sale of Danish butter
n.nd“Damsh eggs.  Viggo, it will be remembered, was
an " expert '’ employed by the I.A.0.S. and some of
his friends were, while he was in the employment of
the Irish Federated Poultry Societies, Ltd., 22 Lincoln
Pla{:e, Dublin, carrying on trade from his address on
their own account. We pub’some light on this matter,
and subsequently Viggo left the country. We suppose
that this ““ expert ”” that came over to teach the bar-
barians of this country their business is now utilising
the knowledge he gained of the Irish trade with a.view
1(:? dumping his Danish dumps. In 1904, whilst this

expert ”” was in the employnent of the I.A.0.S., he
was endeavouring to work a continental agency. We
take credit for being the cause of the exporting of this
imported ‘‘ dump.”  He is now manager of a Copen-
hagep firm that deals in, amongst other things, pickled
Danish eggs; we are sure he would like to pickle us if
he could!

Judging by the proceedings at a meeting of the
Gcx\ex‘al Committee of the Munster Feis, held last weck
in the City Hall, Cork, the prospects before the Feis
are very encouraging. Canon O’Leary; of Castlelyons
An Tazaip Peaoap was unable to be present, but he wrote
a forcible and characteristic letter, which was read at
the meeting. In the course of it he said: —“ T am glad
to see that the wealthy and intellectual people in Cork,
and all over the province, are coming to the aid of the
work. Without such aid, of course, the work would not
continue to go forward. With such aid nothing can
stop the work. It is a great satisfaction to know that
our wealthy and intellectual people believe in the work.
That they see in it the real fundamental force by means
of which a regeneration is to be accomplished.”

The Midland Great Western Railway are announcing
a cheap trip to Dublin in connection with the Irish
Language Procession on next Sunday. Excursion trains
will run to Dublin from Cavan, Edenderry, Sligo, Gal-
way, Loughrea, and other stations. Particulars as to
fares, etc., will be found on posters at stations, or may
be obtained on application to stationmasters.

We have received the syllabus of the County
Monaghan fe1,, which will be held at Castleblaney on
June 30th. Intending competitors should communi-
cate without d:lay to the secretary Paopais Ua
Muirgeans, Donaghmoyne, Carrickmacross.

The Lord Chief Baron, speaking at that notorious
body of intransigentists, the Clongowes Union, stated
for the benefit of the Clongowes boys :—‘ You won’t be
six months in the world before you will know that nearly
a majority of the people that you meet will deny the
existence of God, and will require you to prove it.”” No
doubt, the Lord Chief Baron is speaking from his own
experience. But then perhaps some of the boys whom
he warned. may not keep such “‘ tony ’’ company as his
lordship evidently has experience of ; some of them may
be low down enough to seck their company largely
amongst Papishes, We see from a directory that the
Lord Chief Baron’s Club is the University Club, Dub-
lin. No doubt, the Chief Baron does not anticipate
that any of the Clongowes students will, within six
months of leaving that nest of rebels and mother of
patriots, find their way into a branch of the Gaelic
League!

According to the report in the ¢ Independent *” Alder-
man Kelly declared at Monday’s- meeting of the Dublin
Corporation :— If one of these fellows come to my place
I will get him charged. For the moment I’ll forget I'm
a ‘ Sinn Feiner,” and will take advantage of the British
law.” As Mr. Edward Martyn is taking the British
law against the Kildare Street Club, we take it that he
has quite ceased to remember that he is a ‘‘Sinn
Teiner!” We may shortly expect some total abstinence
man to declare—‘ For the moment I’ll forget that T am:
a teetotaler, and I'll get blind drunk!”

The Home Office campaign against the Kynoch’s in-
dustry in Arklow has been taken up so vigorously by
the daily papers that it is not necessary for us to go ab
length into the matter. The Chairman of Kynoch’s,
Mr. Arthur Chamberlain, is reported as having stated
as an instance of the prejudice against the Irish indus-
try at Arklow that Home Office inspectors told Kynoch’s
that they might make a certain explosive in England,
but that they would not allow them to make it in Ire-
land. The fact that Kynoch’s forced them to permit the
manufacture in Ireland takes nothing from the prejudice
displayed. ~We see that even that rather inert body,
the Dublin Chamber of Commerce, has been moved to
take action in the matter.

Mr. Murphy’s ¢ Independent’’ refers to what it
calls a “‘ piquant’’ question that is to be, or has been,
asked ‘“on the floor of the Touse’ concerning the
paper upon which a British journal called the ““ West-
minster Gazette’’ is printed. Perhaps the ‘ Indepen-
dent”’ would answer the ‘‘ piquant’ question—where
is the paper made on which it is printed? The LEADER,
anyway, is printed on paper made at Ballyclare. How
many ““ highly patriotic”” papers, Irish-Ireland or
Lnglish-Treland, can make a like statement?

The Waterford Feis-—peip Popclimge—is to be
held on Sunday, August 25th, and the syllabus is
already published. Intending competitors should com-
municate with the honorary secretaries, Messrs. E.
Mathews and T. Connolly.

A cutting from Mr. Murphy’s ‘ Independent’ of
May 25 was sent us by a correspondent. Our correspon-
dent wrote on the cutting : —*“ This latest atrocity of the
“Ha’penny Dreadful’ may have escaped, your vigilant
wotice.”  The ‘atrocity,” as our correspondent
rightly calls it, is the ‘* special article’’ in the
“ Dreadful *’ of that date, and is from the pen of some
person who describes himself or herself as ‘‘ Melia Mur-
tagh.” The first paragraph of this insulting drivel in
Mr. Murphy’s ¢ Dreadful *’ is as follows:—

Mos’ peeple like teh have a share av what's goin’, _lmrriu‘
scarletina, hoop'n’ cough, an’ other takin’ discescs that mite tako
yerzel’ after yeh've takin’ any wan av thim. IU's a grate pity
intirely that prosperity is n't infeckshus liko the meesles, or that
yeh can’t be vaxinated wid golden calf limp teh save yoh frum
contractin’ poverty. Ef Irelan’ wor prosperous ivry day ud bo like
‘Whit Mondah at th’ Exhibishun, an’ mos’ ay our ups an’ downs
ud be like a jurney on the Switch Back, excitin’ an’ divartin’ b'ud
not heart-breakin’.  Be that same token, I'm thinkin’ Mr. Willaim
Field, the Membir, shud include the Switch Backs in his plan fur
Nashunal'sin’ the railways of the country. Ef the boys an’ girls
wor carried on the Switch Backs fur a ha’penny a head yeh’d have
a big incrase in the marridge rate. An’ of he included Water
Shoots as part ov the nashunalised system av inland navigashun
be has in his head—an’ ivry wan knows it as well as King
Edward's—an’ reduced the price av a divo teh a ha’penny yeh'd
hardly have an ould maid left in the country 'r an ould bachelor
ayther.

There is more of this baboon sort of drivel. — The
Stage Irishman has met with rotten eggs in America;
but here is the baboon-Irishman placed in honour in Mr.
Murphy’s ‘ Independent.””  Does Mr. Murphy think
that he is increasing his chance of being knighted by
retailing this baboon-Irishman sort of stuff in his paper?
We wonder was it the bogus Paris correspondent who
wrote this stuff in Mr. Murphy’s ‘ Nationalist organ.”’

- - If Edward Martyn has not given a lead to the country
John Sweetman has not been remiss.. Mr. Sweetman
“says that ‘“ Ireland is ripe for this policy of passive
resistance.”’ Well, we cannot answer for Ireland, but
we hope that Mr. Sweetman will be as good as his
adivce, and for a beginning refuse to pay his income and
other taxes, also his rates, and, at least, go heroically
to gaol for his passive principles. Mr. Sweetman says
that all who wish Ireland to prosper should now—what
do you think, expectant reader?—join his party!
Amongst other things he says we should ‘‘ prepare for
the day of England’s weakness which will always be
Ireland’s opportunity.”” Truly, raimeds dies hard! We
thought that that sort of thing had been very nearly
killed since the Boer war. England was weak and hard

In our notice in last issue of the ‘“ Aonach’’ at the
Tather Mathew Iall, Church St., we accidentally over-
looked including Sinn F'ein, Ltd., amongst the exhi-
bitors in the Irish industrial section. We are now glad
to repair the omission, as the well-knovyn Camden St.
firm has, since its inception, been conspicuously identi-
fied with the movement in favour of Irish manufacture.
The products of no less than seven Irish industries were
represented in the exhibits of Sinn Fein, Litd., at the
Aonach. Their display included walnut tables made by
0’Dea, Stafford St., wicker chairs from Castlecomer,
kitchen chairs made by Hands, Usher’s Island, wool
rugs and mats from St. Joseph’s Blind Asylum, chairs
manufactured by Alesbury Bros., Edenderry, ete.

So the University Bill as well as the Council Bill is
dead. Evidently the forces represented by the grand-
son of Smith O’Brien, Prof. Gwynn, were too strong for
the ¢ Liberal”” Government to face. This particular
grandson of Smith O’Brien, it will be remembered, was
recently on a spouting tour to the Big Brother in the
interests of Queen Elizabeth’s foundation in Ireland.
We may expect Dr. McGrath, one of the Secretaries of
the ‘“‘loyal’”’ Royal, Mr. Charles E. Martin and other stal-
warts, to be driven to desperation now, and we will not
be surprised if we hear any day that these heroes, led
by Chief Baron Palles, are marching with muskets over
their shoulders on Dublin Castle! We trust they will
not do anything over rash. In the words of Mr. Wil-
liam Murphy’s ‘‘ Independent,” we would say to ‘ex-
tremists like the members of the Clongowes Union, let
us press ‘‘ our demand for reform vigorcusly but not
immoderately. Thus we shall achieve—sooner, per-
haps, than may be expected—the realisation of our
cherished aspirations.”” = We don’t like to invoke the
aid of Dublin Castle, but we feel it our bounden duty
to warn the police authorities—and, indeed, the mili-
tary authorities—of the necessity of keeping close watch
on Clongowes College and on the residence of the Chief
Baron. At least two detectives might be told off to
keep special eyes on Dr. McGrath, the Papish Secretary
of the Royal University. These extremists are like
sparks in a gunpowder magazine at the present moment!

On Monday, 10th inst., the Phibshoro’ Bazaar will
open in the Rotunda. The Bazaar will continue until
the following Saturday. We are glad to note an Irish
industry stall amongst the features announced.

TO “THE FLOOR OF THE HAGUE!”

T is rather hard lines on Mr. Edward Martyn’s
party that the Nationalist M.P.’s have so miser-
ably failed on the main chance, and that the Conven-
tion has ignominiously kicked out the Bill that their
pressure, eloquence and tactics induced the ‘‘ Liberals "’
to offer this country. For in the lull that follows the
Parliamentary slump Mr. Martyn’s party is called upon
for a song. We can see Mr. Martyn in our minds’ eye
all blushes and embarrassment as he hears the ominous
ery—‘‘ Silence, gentlemen, for Mr. Martyn’s song.”
We venture to prophesy that Mr. Martyn will have
such an attack of hoarseness that he cannot oblige.
Now the Parliamentarians are down in the dust, and
Mr. Martyn’s party are asked to do something besides
snar]l at them! What do they propose to do?

So far their great proposal is a cause of laughter.
The official organ of Mr. Martyn’s party cannot remain
silent like the President, for a weekly paper must oblige
with a song every week, no matter what happens.

The LeapEr and its readers pioneered the Industrial
Revival ; they helped to popularise the Irish Langauge
Movement and to dethrone the politicians from the
position of practical monopolisers of nationality. ~We
ridiculed the pretensions of the Empees ‘ fighting the
battle of Treland on the floor of the British House of
Commons.””  Someone has remarked that the Boers
could fight and run away to cover and fight again, but
that once his officers let Tommy Atkins take leg bail
there was no stopping him until he dropped from ex-
haustion. We founded the real Sinn Fein movement,
and helped to work it to some purpose; Mr. Martyn’s
eoterie attempted to take it under their exclusive wing,
and succeeded in making themselves ridiculous. It
does not at all follow that: because, the pretensious wings
of the Empees needed clipping that they ware to be
regarded as monsters, nor did it follow because ‘“the
Hoor of the British House of Commons” nceded the
saving application of ridicule that it became as unten-
able as Spion Kop. But some people ride ideas to
where beggars are supposed to ride horses.

The real Sinn Fein idea may be briefly stated as the
promotion by effective means of the language, industries
and political power of Ireland and the promotion of tem-
perance, toleration, backbone and brain. There is no
royal road to all these things. Like life, they demand
K ormous amount of attention .to dull and prosy rou-

but there are always people who would like life
ade up of a series of sensational shocks. Some
in for dissipation in politics as others do in

The *“ floor of the House” had its day of glamour;
now Mr. Martyn’s excellent tr.oupe!?f broad comedians
by way of answering the ql_xeslnonT what next?’—are

Tf debating points were ‘‘Long Toms’’ we might,
have ITome Rule as the Boers have it. Some of our
neo-green people appear to think they are. A year or
50 ago one of our neo-green papers worked 1tsg][ up into
a gf‘o.ab pitch of cxciteme_n“& on the assumption that a
point concerning The ;[\H"Ig, Lol‘ds and Commons of
Treland *’ placed the Unionists in a corner! Much the
Unionists care for corners guarded by syllogisms—real or
imitation. Brother Campbell, X.C., and Walter Long
would think more of one good threatening blackthorn
than of fifty syllogisms. But our neo-greens do not see
that.

The latest brilliant idea of the neo-green party is
to go to “ the floor of the Hague.”” The oﬂ‘gcial organ
of Mr. Martyn’s party suggests it in a certain—a very
certain—contingency to those who believe in Parliamen-
tarianism. By the way, believing in Parliamentarianism
seems to be freely and fallaciously represented by some
as necessarily believing that Parliamentfary action can,
of itself, obtain Home Rule for Ircland! The proposi-
tion is, of course, absurd. For our part we believed in
Parliamentarianism, and believe in it still, as a means
of procuring, for instance, a Bill to close up the public-
houses on St. Patrick’s Day. We helieve in it as a
means of amending the School Attendance Act so far as
it needs amending ; we believe in it, and so does Alder-
man Cole, as a means of dealing with the University
question, and, with the Gaelic League, we believe in it
as a means of helping to get—out of our own money—
the fees for Irish teaching in the primary schools. But
Mr. Martyn’s party, as an organised entity, though in-
dividuals of the party apparently do not agree, appear
to regard anyone upholding the party as committing
himself to the view that the party can get Home Rule!
Mr. Martyn’s party organ asks:— If the Irish Parlia-
mentary Party cannot achieve lome Rule at Westmin-
ster, why are they there?’ We have already suggested
the answer to that question, and even Alderman Cole
held that they might have got a University Bill!

Mr. Martyn’s party’s official organ suggests that the
Irish Parliamentary Party can at once re-introduce the
Home Rule Bill of 1893, and that the Liberal Govern-
ment should either accept the Bill or ““ publicly admit
its perfidy and betrayal of Ireland.”” TFor our part,
as we believe already that the ‘‘ Liberals”’ are frauds,
and will do no more for Ireland than they are com-
pelled by pressure to do, we would not waste time on
such pantomime and child’s play. For the *“ Liberals,’’
having, as they declare, no ““ mandate,”” would refuse to
pass the Bill of 1893 ; we could call them names and fling
““ perfidy *’ and ““ betrayers ”’ at them which would not ‘
hurt them in the least. But Mr. Martyn’s party has a
master-stroke ready for the Liberals if they refused to
pass the Bill of 1893, The Trish Parliamentary Party,
they suggest, could then adopt the resolution—we have
italicised Ireland’s green, immortal ‘“ Long Tom,” the
word ““ resolution,’”” and we admit we smiled as we did
so—the party could then adopt ‘‘ the vesolution of the
Hungarian Deputies who, in 1861, refused to sit in the
Imperial Parliament.”” 'This Martynite ‘ Long Tom ”
is as follows:—‘‘ We protest against the exercise, on
the part of the Imperial Parliament, of any legislative
or other power over Hungary in any relation whatso-
ever; we declare that we will not send representatives"
to the Imperial Parliament, and further, that any elec-
tion by other instrumentality will be an attack on our
constitution, and wa declare that, any person alected by
such means cannot in any respect represent Hungary.’’
The official organ of Mr. Martyn’s party proceeds:—

After ““the floor of the House ’—the “ floor of the
Hague.”” What an anti-climax to the dveams indulged
in by many of Mr. Martyn’s young and trusting fol-
lowers twenty golden months ago! They have been
led on to worry Mr. Redmond’s party, helped by the
¢ “ Irish ’ Times’’ and other British papers, and now it is

Ilouse has given way in part, and John Redmond has
fallen down. He has one consolation, anyway—he has
fallen on top of Edward Martyn! The country had
better grasp the idea of strengthening Ireland rather
than of weakening England, of supporting Irish indus-
tries and increasing Irish productivity and Irish effi-
ciency rather than indulging in cheap and somewhat
pernicious talk about boycotting England. Talk of boy-
cotting England in all probability would end almost
wholly in talk on the part of those who indulged in it,
whilst it v only be calculated to aggravate unneces-

this country against Irish industries—and Ireland, we

would be made of it in England. At the least, Ireland
ought to show as much skill and calculation and restraint
in playing her national game as a party coming home
in a train from a fair display in playing a game of spoil

WHAT CAN WE DO WITH OUR CAPITAL ?

™ AN any of your readers suggest to me any way to
use a little surplus money in Ireland without
losing it—any industry to start, or assist; any livelihood
in which to set up one or more people, so as to give some-
one an opening in life, to increase industry and employ-
ment, and thus benefit the county to this small extent,
while, at the same time, keeping my principal and in-
terest-moderately secure?

I have a few hundreds to invest in excess of the re-
quirements of my own occupation, and instead of buy-
ing Coats’ or calicoes with them, I would prefer to have
them employed at home, if I could get the same interest,
the same security, and the same case of realising them
again if necessary.

If I take shares in Guinness’s, or some Dublin
drapery, or Irish railway or bank, I am only exchang-
ing with other people; I am adding nothing to the
capital employed in Treland, My money will not be
helping these Irish enterprises a bit, for they are in full
‘working order, and will run as well on the other man’s
money as on mine, and will not develop or extend tlgqm-
selves; or spend or earn a penny more by my acquiring
some of their stock.

“Start an industry,” says someone, ‘‘or get up a
Jocal company.”  This raupedis, *“ Let somebody else

patriotism of country shopkeepers and monied men in
not starting factories.  The few thousands that the
people of a country town could put together would be
utterly insufficient to start even a small industry, and
no small manufacture can be run with profit, at least,
by a company. It is only a large enterprise, furnished
with the newest labour-saving machinery, and able to
utilise to the full all the time of its employees—with ex-
tensive enough production to minimise its cost, ahd a
market large enough to dispose of all its output—that can
pessibly compete in price with the giant enterprises,
run on these lines, that hold the markets of the world.

The only small factories that survive are those that
have been established securely in the old times of small
wages and large profits before modern economic condi-
tions gave such an overwhelning advantage to the large
industrial concerns, and which are still run by the one-
man owner whose watchfulness, economy and untiring
energy in behalf of his own sole livelihood saves the
difference between profit and loss in his trading account.

But, apart from the insufficiency of capital, it is, T
fear, the merest nonsense for a body of country shop-
keepers and farmers and professional men to combine
to start and work an industry at which they are all
amateurs. Each of them has his own business to inind,
and needs all his time and energy to mind it. They
know nothing whatever of the technical and highly pro-
fessional trade they are undertaking, and none of them
can possibly, without neglecting his own business, which
is of much more importance to him, give the new enter-
prise the continual attention and supervision it needs,
or can push its sales as earnestly as is necessary for a
new article or dealer to invade the markets with any
success.

““ Get a good manager ’’ is the world’s ready answer
to this difficulty ; but the good manager is in a better
job, which he will not leave except for a salary which
the moderate trade of the new factory could not pos-
sibly pay him. It is only the inefficient managers who
are out of employment, and are ready to answer adver-
tisements.

The only manufacture or trade which I can see to
have the slightest chance of success is that started and
run by one man who has knowledge and training
in it ; whose sole interest and occupation it is, and whose
whole livelihood and means depend on its success; and
the big joint-stock manufactories which command the
world’s markets today are mostly growths of a one-
man enterprise of a former generation.

A local investment within the compass of my money
would be house-building ; but what is the use of building
houses in a country town? Who is to occupy them?
There are houses enough for our inhabitants; our popu-
lation is not increasing ; trade is not improving, and the
outlook for it is worse with the growing poverty and
untrustworthiness of so many of the farmers, and the
decrease of tillage and of employment for labourers.

The example of over-built Belfast is a warning to
house-building speculators. Those who havée built
houses in country towns as an investment in recent
years, and have got them set, say that they pay only
six_ per cent. gross, and the increasing difficulty of
obtaining rent and the probable extension of town
tenants’ legislation, however desirable in the general
interests, is not at all encouraging to would-be investors.

And as for the building of small, cheap houses, all
private enterprise in this department has been killed
by the Labourers Acts. The providing of labourers’
cottages under these Acts is, T think, the greatest and
best social reform of the last twenty years, next to, and
not far behind, the land legislation ; and one need waste
no sympathy on the few individual house-owners who
suffer in income from so beneficent a change. But the
country capitalist cannot be blamed for not entering
into competition with the District Councils, and build-
ing houses at a cost of 2. 6d. a week interest (the
plainest, decent houses cannot be built for less) to set
them at a shilling.

The one and only investment which I can see -for
moderate sums of money is the land.

you would think, would be scarcely the glace you would
go to learn Irish. Constant struggle for their rights
has, however, got the Irishmen of the city to a temper
that you can generally depend on to stand fire, and of
those of them who have tackled the learning of Irish, a
bigger proportion than in perhaps any other of our
towns, are in earnest. The League’s premises, An
Cpaob Ruwo, are quickly blossoming into evidence of
that earnestness. The Language Collection is, a.nd the
Language Procession (for which Belfast is running an
excursion and bringing a tableau) will be, further
evidences,  As for the studying of the language, the
League examinations now in progress are opening the
eyes of the examiners to the fact that there are now in
Belfast what are practically native speakers of Irish who
have never been to the (taedhealtacht. Speaking as
one of those examiners, I can guarantee for the pro-
posed Middle Irish Class, not alone from amongst the
teachers, but even from the learners of the city, quite
exceptional material to start work with.

As regards the library, not alone will such an institu-
tion be indispensable to the new class, but it will add
a sense of security, of fixity of intention, as it were, to
the League here. In most League branches throughout
the country, meeting spasmodically as they do in school-
rooms or wherever they can find shelter, not only can
you not feel that the League has taken root in Ireland,
but you cannot help feeling the exact contrary. Up to
this, of course, Leaguers have been for the most part
hardly what you would call business men, and after all
it is speculation and business to take permanent rooms
or open a library for a branch. Belfast was long
enough without the rooms, but now that she has them
she is quickly proving what an advantage the like are.
T have no doubt but that her venture into the field of old
Trish will be similarly successful.

revolt against existing systems in Ireland, whereas as

stration of the system. In the index of the pamphlet

them the power of appointing and dismissing teachers,

says it would not be just or expedient for the legisla-
ture to deprive managers of these powers, but in the
same breath he proposes another way by which the
same end is to be attained. e invites the Irish people
to raise local rates for educational purposes, and” he
suggests that any ambitious, recalcitrant managers who
would refuse to give up their present powers of appoint-
ment and dismissal should be punished by withholding
from them any portion of these rates. Strange enough,
the author does not protest against the financial rob-
bery of Irish education by the Treasury. He rather
sncers at the idea of the University Question being a
burning one, and suggests that -the settlement of it
should be left to his new Education Council. His great
concern was evidently how to reform Primary and In-

termediate  Education, to start local rates,

thus eventually put an end to the present managerial
